metadata.summary: Notion PM combines task tracking and docs using databases with relations and rollups. Use a single Tasks database with Sprint as a relation, never create per-sprint databases.

content.title: Notion PM
content.summary: Notion PM uses interconnected databases (Projects, Tasks, Sprints) with relation and rollup fields to build a customizable agile workspace. Tasks must exist as individual database pages — not as sub-bullets inside sprint documents — to be queryable via the API. Use a single Tasks database with Sprint as a relation field; never create a new database per sprint, which fragments history and breaks velocity computation.

why.p: Notion combines task tracking and documentation in one workspace, eliminating the PM-tool + wiki context switch. Its flexible database structure adapts to evolving processes faster than Jira or Linear. Stakeholders get read-only access via a shareable page without creating PM accounts. The Notion API supports full CRUD on pages and databases, enabling agent-driven sprint planning, standup digests, and sprint closure workflows.

list.item: Small agile team (2–10 people) wanting sprint planning, backlog, and docs in one tool
When sprint retrospectives, meeting notes, and task tracking need inline cross-references
When workflow is still evolving — Notion databases restructure faster than Jira or Linear
Solopreneur or micro-team where full sprint tooling (velocity charts, burndowns) is overkill
When stakeholders need read-only project status via shareable page without PM tool accounts
Teams with mature Scrum practices needing native burndown charts, velocity tracking, and sprint analytics
High-velocity engineering teams with 10+ members — database performance degrades with large datasets
When issues need tight Git/PR integration (auto-close on merge, branch naming)
Organizations needing SOC2/HIPAA compliant issue tracking with field-level audit logs

section.name: database-design
section.title: Notion Database Design for Agile PM
section.p: Projects and Tasks database schemas, relation and rollup field patterns, and formula examples for sprint metrics.

rule.statement: Use a single Tasks database with Sprint as a relation field; never create a new database per sprint.
rule.rationale: Per-sprint databases fragment task history and prevent cross-sprint velocity computation; a single Tasks database with a Sprint relation supports all views and rollups without data migration.

rule.statement: Keep each task as a separate database page, not as a sub-bullet inside a sprint document.
rule.rationale: Only database pages are queryable via the Notion API; sub-bullets cannot be filtered, sorted, or updated by agents.

rule.statement: Limit database properties to 15–20 maximum; archive unused properties rather than accumulating them.
rule.rationale: Complex formulas and many properties slow Notion database loading; unused properties add visual noise and agent confusion.

rule.statement: Tasks database required properties: Task (title), Status (status type — not select), Priority (select: Urgent/High/Medium/Low), Assignee (person), Project (relation), Sprint (relation), Estimate (number), Due Date (date), Tags (multi_select), Blocked By (self-relation).
rule.rationale: Status type (vs select) enables Notion's built-in kanban grouping; self-relation on Blocked By enables dependency tracking without a separate database.

antipattern.description: Using select property type for Status instead of the native status type.
antipattern.reason: Select type loses Notion's built-in grouping behavior (not_started/in_progress/complete) that kanban and board views rely on.

rule.statement: Use bidirectional relations between Tasks and Projects; add rollup properties on Projects for: task count, completed task count, and total estimate sum.
rule.rationale: Bidirectional relations allow navigation both ways; rollups give Projects a live progress indicator without formulas that break when relations change.

example.note: Projects database rollup: "Completed Tasks" — relation: Tasks, property: Status, calculation: Count values, filter: Done. This gives live completion count per project.

rule.statement: Use formulas for derived display fields only (days until due, overdue flag, priority emoji) — never use formulas for metrics that require cross-database aggregation.
rule.rationale: Notion formulas cannot aggregate across databases; cross-database metrics require relation + rollup chains, which are more reliable and auto-update.

section.name: views-automations
section.title: Notion Views and Automations
section.p: Board, table, timeline, and calendar view configurations; native automation patterns; Zapier/n8n integration.

rule.statement: Create four purpose-driven views per database: Board (sprint execution, group by Status), Table (all tasks, sort by priority), Timeline (roadmap, group by Project), Calendar (deadlines, filter: Status not Done).
rule.rationale: Each view serves a distinct PM function; proliferating views with overlapping purposes creates confusion about which view is authoritative.

example.note: Board view: filter Sprint = Current Sprint, group by Status, show Assignee/Priority/Estimate/Due Date properties on cards.

antipattern.description: Creating a new view for every sprint or team member.
antipattern.reason: Views multiply without a governance policy; use filters on shared views instead of creating per-person or per-sprint views.

rule.statement: Use native Notion automations only for property-triggered actions: set Completion Date when Status is Done, notify Assignee when Priority is Urgent, set Status to Backlog on page creation.
rule.rationale: Native automations are limited to property triggers — they cannot run on a schedule. For time-based jobs (daily standup digest, sprint closure), use n8n, Make, or a cron-based agent.

rule.statement: Never delete sprint pages — archive them by setting a Status property to "Completed". Deleted sprint pages break any task's Sprint relation field that pointed to them.
rule.rationale: Notion relation fields that point to deleted pages become null silently; rollup properties that count sprint tasks stop working. Archived pages preserve all existing relations.

rule.statement: Use n8n (self-hostable) or Make for time-triggered Notion workflows: daily standup digest (query tasks in sprint where Status = Blocked or In Progress → post to Slack), sprint closure (compute metrics → fill Sprint page).
rule.rationale: n8n is compatible with the NERO stack and can be self-hosted; it handles the Notion API rate limit (3 req/sec) with built-in retry logic.

section.name: agent-usage
section.title: Notion Agent Integration and Gotchas
section.p: Agentic sprint cycle automation, recommended subagents, API rate limits, relation resolution gotchas, and Python sprint assignment script.

rule.statement: Notion API rate limit is 3 requests per second per integration token; paginate all database queries with start_cursor (max 100 items per request regardless of page_size); add 350ms delay between write requests during bulk operations.
rule.rationale: 3 req/sec limit is hit quickly during sprint planning with 100 tasks; 350ms delay stays safely under the limit; pagination is mandatory — Notion never returns more than 100 items per query regardless of page_size setting.

rule.statement: After writing a relation field, wait 1–2 seconds before querying rollup properties that depend on it — rollups recalculate asynchronously after relation writes.
rule.rationale: Rollup recalculation is not synchronous with the relation write response; an immediate query returns stale values that do not reflect the newly assigned sprint or project.

rule.statement: Resolve relation values to human-readable text by fetching related pages via pages.retrieve — the API returns [{id: "page-uuid"}], not page titles.
rule.rationale: Sprint names, assignee names, and project titles must be resolved from page IDs before producing any human-readable report; UUID arrays are meaningless to report consumers.

section.name: sprint-assignment
section.title: Sprint Assignment Script

example.note: 350ms delay between writes keeps under the 3 req/sec Notion rate limit; function is called only after human approval of the sprint plan.

section.reference: Prompt for agent to select and assign backlog tasks to a sprint

list.item: notion-sprint-planner: reads backlog, ranks by priority/estimate, selects tasks fitting capacity, posts plan to Slack for human approval before saving to Notion.
notion-standup-agent: daily; queries sprint tasks where Status = In Progress or Blocked; posts structured digest to Slack with Notion page links.
notion-sprint-closer: at sprint end; reads Sprint page rollups, fills Metrics table, sets Sprint to Completed, moves incomplete tasks to next sprint.
Use a single Tasks database with Sprint as a relation field — never create a new database per sprint, which fragments history and breaks velocity computation.
Define the Sprint page as the source of truth for sprint dates; use rollup properties on the Tasks database to derive "Is Current Sprint" rather than filtering by a hardcoded sprint name.
Keep each task as a separate database page, not a sub-bullet inside a sprint document — only pages are queryable via the Notion API.
Archive sprint pages (set a Status property to "Completed") rather than deleting — deleted pages break any task relation that pointed to them.
For capacity planning, add an "Estimate" number property to every task and enforce it via a formula that flags missing estimates — agents need this field to compute sprint fit.
Paginate all database queries with start_cursor — Notion returns max 100 items per request regardless of page_size parameter.
Notion API returns relation property values as [{id: "page-uuid"}] — agents must follow up with pages.retrieve to get the page title (sprint name, assignee name) for human-readable output.
After creating or updating a relation, the rollup that depends on it may take 1–2 seconds to recalculate — agents querying rollup values immediately after a write may see stale data.
Sprint "current" filtering: Notion has no native "current sprint" concept; agents must compare sprint page date properties with today using a formula or a filter with date.after / date.before.
The status property type (introduced 2022) behaves differently from select in API responses — always check the property type before reading status.name vs select.name.
Native Notion automations run asynchronously and cannot be triggered by the API — do not rely on them firing within a specific time window in an agent pipeline.
Human checkpoint required before bulk sprint assignment (adding 30 tasks to a sprint via API) — review the plan output before calling assign_to_sprint.

template.purpose: Notion task page template with context, acceptance criteria, and updates log
Sprint page template with goal, metrics, backlog, standups, and retrospective
Prompt for agent to select and assign backlog tasks to a sprint
